1: Nāsû rĕšāʿîm wĕ'ên rōdēp = “The wicked flee when no one pursues” — the guilty conscience creates its own pursuers. Wĕṣaddîqîm kikpîr yibṭāḥ = “but the righteous are as bold as a young lion.” Kĕpîr = a young, full-grown lion at peak strength — the image is not of aggression but of confidence: the righteous person does not bolt because there is nothing to flee from. Moral innocence and YHWH-trust together produce this unshakable bearing.
2: Bĕpeša' ʾereṣ rabbîm śārêhā = “In the transgression of a land many are its rulers” — national sin fragments political authority; too many competing rulers is itself a symptom of moral disorder. Wĕbĕ'ādām mêbîn yōdēaʿ — disputed: yādaʿ (H3045, dispute_level=2) with the range “know / understand / perceive / experience.” The MKT renders “through a man of understanding and knowledge its stability endures” — one person of discernment can stabilize what collective sin has shattered.
3: Geber-rāš wĕ'ōšēq dallîm = “A poor man who oppresses the poor” — the inversion is the point: the one with no resources to oppress still does so. Māṭār sōḥēp wĕ'ên lāḥem = “like a driving/sweeping rain that leaves no food” — the heavy rains that strip the soil and wash away crops rather than watering them. The rain is violent, not nourishing: destructive without remedy.
4: ʿŌzĕbê tôrāh yĕhallĕlû rāšāʿ = “Those who forsake the law (tôrāh) praise the wicked” — tôrāh is instruction/law, the foundational term for divine teaching. Those who abandon it lose the moral framework that enables them to identify wickedness as wicked; they end up praising it instead. Wĕšōmĕrê tôrāh yitgārû bām = “but those who keep the law contend with them” — law-keepers resist the wicked by definition.
5: ʾAnšê-rāʿ lō' yābînû mišpāṭ = “Evil men do not understand justice” — moral failing produces epistemic incapacity; the wicked cannot perceive right order because wickedness deforms the perceiving faculty. Wĕmĕbaqqĕšê YHWH yābînû kōl = “but those who seek YHWH understand everything.” YHWH (H3068, dispute_level=4) — the personal divine name; “LORD” in standard translations conceals this. Seeking YHWH is the comprehensive epistemological orientation that opens the full range of understanding.
6: 'Better is the poor man who walks in his integrity (bĕtummô) than the one who is perverse in his ways (wĕ'iqqēš dĕrākayim), even if he is rich' — tōm (integrity, wholeness) is the inner coherence of the person whose conduct matches their values. ʾIqqēš (crooked/twisted) is the antonym: a person whose paths are doubled/divided — saying one thing, doing another. The verse weighs character against wealth and finds wealth lacking.
7: Nôṣēr tôrāh bēn mêbîn = “Whoever keeps the law is a wise son” — nāṣar (keep/guard/watch) — the watchful guarding of the tôrāh. Wĕrōʿēh zôlĕlîm yaklîm ʾābîw = “but a companion of gluttons (zôlĕlîm) brings shame on his father” — zālal (be loose/worthless/prodigal) encompasses both excess eating and moral looseness; the gluttonous companion is the fool-company warning of Proverbs 13:20.
8: Marbeh hônô bĕneśek ûbĕtarbît = “Whoever increases wealth by interest (neśek) and dishonest gain” — neśek (bite, interest on a loan) and tarbît (increase/usury) are the twin commercial vices forbidden in the Torah (Leviticus 25:36-37). Lĕḥônen dallîm yiqbĕṣennû = “gathers it for one who will be generous to the poor” — divine irony: the ill-gotten wealth is providentially redirected to the generous. YHWH ultimately controls the redistribution of wealth.
9: Mēsîr ʾoznô miššĕmōaʿ tôrāh = “Whoever turns his ear away from hearing the law” — the deliberate aversion of the ear from tôrāh. Gam-tĕpillātô tôʿēbāh = “even his prayer is detestable” (tôʿēbāh, abomination). The shocking inversion: the person who ignores divine instruction when it suits them cannot expect their requests to be received. Selective listening to God is answered by selective hearing of prayer.
10: Mašgeh yĕšārîm bĕderek rāʿ = “Whoever leads the righteous astray in an evil way” — šāgāh (lead astray, cause to wander) describes deliberate misdirection of those who are upright. Bišḥûtô hûʾ-yippôl = “into his own pit he will fall” — the trap prepared for others becomes the agent's own snare. Ûtĕmîmîm yinḥălû-ṭôb = “but the blameless will inherit good.”
11: Ḥākām bĕ'ênāyw ʾîš ʿāšîr = “A rich man is wise in his own eyes” — wealth creates the illusion of wisdom by providing access to results (people comply, projects succeed) that real wisdom would also produce. Wĕdal mêbîn yaḥqĕrennû = “but a poor man with discernment will see right through him.” Ḥāqar (search/investigate/probe) — the discerning poor person probes and exposes the hollow wisdom of the wealthy fool.
12: Baʿălôṣ ṣaddîqîm rabbāh tipʾāret = “When the righteous triumph, great is the glory/rejoicing” — tipʾāret (glory/beauty/splendor) describes the flourishing state of a community under righteous leadership. Ûbĕqûm rĕšāʿîm yĕḥuppas ʾādām = “but when the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding” — the social compression of wickedness in power: public life becomes dangerous, people withdraw. See v28 for the mirror image.
13: Mĕkasseh pĕšāʿāyw lō' yaṣlîaḥ = “Whoever conceals his sins will not prosper” — kāsāh (cover/conceal) here is self-protective hiding, not the generous “covering” of Proverbs 17:9. Self-concealment of sin prevents the breakthrough that would lead to mercy. Ûmôdeh wĕ'ōzēb yĕruḥām = “but whoever confesses (yādāh) and forsakes them will find mercy.” Yādāh (confess/praise) is the same root as thanksgiving: confession is a kind of honoring of God's authority over the sin. The closest wisdom equivalent to the NT confession theology of 1 John 1:9.
14: ʾAšrê ʾādām mĕpahēd tāmîd = “Happy is the one who fears (pāḥad) the LORD continually” — pāḥad is the strong trembling-fear, more vivid than yārēʾ (the standard “fear of YHWH”); it suggests an ongoing disposition of awe before YHWH's holiness. Ûmaqšeh libbô yippōl bĕrāʿāh = “but whoever hardens his heart (qāšāh-lēb) will fall into calamity.” The hard heart is Pharaoh's condition (Exodus 7:3); applying it to the wisdom setting grounds the two trajectories of Proverbs in covenantal history.
15: ʾAryēh nōhēm wĕdōb šōqēq = “A roaring lion or a charging/rushing bear (dōb šōqēq)” — šāqaq (rush headlong, roam recklessly) describes the bear's predatory charging motion. Mōšēl rāšāʿ ʿal-ʿam dāl = “so is a wicked ruler over a poor people” — the wicked ruler over the powerless combines both predatory animals: the lion's roar (intimidation) and the bear's charge (violence). The poor people have no means of resistance.
16: Nāgîd ḥăsar tĕbûnôt = “A ruler lacking discernment (tĕbûnôt)” — tĕbûnāh (discernment/insight) is the practical wisdom to understand situations. Wĕrab maʿăśaqqôt = “and a great oppressor.” The connection is diagnostic: lack of discernment and oppression are linked — the ruler who cannot think cannot govern and so resorts to force. Śônēʾ beṣaʿ yaʾărîk yāmîm = “but whoever hates unjust gain will prolong his days.”>
17: ʾĀdām ʿāšûq bĕdam-nepeš = “A man burdened with the guilt of blood (dam-nepeš) of another person” — nepeš (H5315) here is “life/person” — the whole living person whose blood has been shed. ʿAd-bôr yānûs ʾal-yitměkû-bô = “will flee to the pit; let no one help him.” The blood-guilt pursues the killer to Sheol (bôr, the pit/grave); the prohibition on helping him is a community protection against harboring a fugitive blood-guiltsman.
18: Hôlēk tāmîm yiwwāšēaʿ = “Whoever walks with integrity (tōm) will be saved” — yāšaʿ (be saved/delivered) — the salvation vocabulary applied to moral integrity; the root is the same as yēšûʿāh (salvation/Joshua/Jesus). Wĕniʿqaš dĕrākayim yippôl bĕ'aḥat = “but the one who is crooked in his ways (niʿqaš) will fall at once.”> Niʿqaš (crooked/twisted-double paths) is the literal image of walking a zig-zag path.
19: ʿŌbēd ʾadmātô yiśbaʿ lāḥem = “Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread” — agricultural labor as the paradigm of productive work. Ûmĕraddēp rêqîm yiśbaʿ rîš = “but whoever chases empty pursuits (rêqîm, empty things) will have plenty of poverty” — the bitter irony: the diligent worker has plenty of bread; the idle chaser has plenty of poverty. The parallel structure lets the word “plenty” (yiśbaʿ, be sated) carry the contrast.
20: ʾÎš ʾĕmûnôt rab-bĕrākôt = “A faithful man (ʾĕmûnôt, faithfulnesses) will abound with blessings” — ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/reliability) is the character quality of consistent trustworthiness. Wĕʾāṣ lĕhāʿăšîr lō' yinnāqeh = “but whoever is eager/hasty to get rich will not go unpunished” — the urgency of wealth-seeking corrupts the faithful character; get-rich-quick schemes require cutting ethical corners.
21: Hakkēr-pānîm lō'-ṭôb = “To show partiality (hakkēr pānîm, recognize/acknowledge a face) is not good” — the idiom for favoritism: granting favorable treatment because you recognize (know) someone. Ûʿal-pat leḥem yipšaʿ-gāber = “yet for a mere piece of bread a man will commit a wrong.” The smallness of the inducement is the point: partiality is so deeply embedded that it takes almost nothing to activate it.
22: Niḇhāl lĕhôn ʾîš rāʿ ʿāyin = “A man who is eager/hasty for wealth is a man of an evil eye” — raʿ ʿāyin (evil eye) is the Hebrew idiom for stinginess/greed (contrast ṭôb ʿāyin, good eye = generosity, in Proverbs 22:9). Wĕlō' yēdaʿ kî-ḥeser yĕbōʾennû = “and does not know (yādaʿ, H3045) that poverty is coming for him.”> His greed blinds him to the trajectory his greed creates.
23: Môkîaḥ ʾādām ʾaḥărāy ḥēn yimṣā' = “Whoever rebukes a person will afterward find more favor (ḥēn) than the one who flatters” — ḥēn (H2580, dispute_level=2): grace/favor/charm/kindness. The dispute is whether it describes the approval the rebuker receives from the rebuked person in retrospect, or from the community. The MKT “more favor” is right: honest rebuke, though painful initially, earns genuine relational capital that flattery never builds.
24: Gōzēl ʾābîw wĕʾimmô wĕʾōmēr ʾên-pāšaʿ = “Whoever robs his father or mother and says ‘There is nothing wrong with it’” — gāzal (rob/seize by violence) applied to parents; the rationalizing speech ‘ên pāšaʿ’ (no transgression) is the hallmark of hardened conscience. Ḥābēr hûʾ lĕʾîš mašḥît = “is a partner to a man who destroys” — the rationalizer is classified with the destroyer; the self-justification does not change the moral category.
25: Raḥab-nepeš yĕgāreh mādon = “A greedy person (raḥab-nepeš, wide/expanded of appetite) stirs up strife” — nepeš (H5315) here = appetite/desire; the “wide-souled” person whose desires have no limit. Ûbôṭēaḥ ʿal-YHWH yĕdušshan = “but whoever trusts in the LORD will prosper/be fat (dāšan).” The one who trusts YHWH (rather than expanding their own appetite) receives the prosperity the greedy person grasps after.
26: Bôṭēaḥ bĕlibbô hûʾ kĕsîl = “Whoever trusts his own heart is a fool” — the lēb (heart/mind) as the center of reasoning and willing; self-trust of this kind is specifically named folly. Wĕhôlēk bĕḥokmāh hûʾ yimmālēṭ = “but whoever walks in wisdom will be kept safe” — yimmālēṭ (escape/be delivered). The contrast is between two sources of guidance: self-referential trust vs. wisdom-shaped walking.
27: Nôtēn lārāš ʾên mahsôr = “Whoever gives to the poor will not lack (mahsôr)” — the generosity paradox: giving creates no deficit for the giver. Wĕmaʿlîm ʿênāyw rab-mĕʾērôt = “but whoever hides his eyes from them will get many curses.” Maʿlîm ʿênāyw = averting the gaze — the deliberate non-seeing that enables inaction. The curses are both social (from the poor) and divine (for ignoring covenant obligation).
28: Bĕqûm rĕšāʿîm yissātēr ʾādām = “When the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding” — the public withdrawal described in v12 now bookends the chapter. Ûbĕʾābĕdām yirbû ṣaddîqîm = “but when the wicked perish, the righteous multiply.”> The righteous community's growth is directly linked to the removal of wicked power: oppression suppresses flourishing; its removal allows multiplication. The chapter closes with this political-theological vision.
